Many developers choose DHTMLX Gantt because it combines strong performance with extensive customization options. Licensing has also been one of its advantages. For years, the library was available under the GPL license, making it a practical choice for many open-source projects. More recently, DHTMLX introduced a Community Edition under the more permissive MIT license, giving developers greater flexibility. In this article, we’ll break down the available licensing options, explain what changed with the Community Edition, and discuss when it makes sense to move to the commercial PRO edition.
GPL License: Still Available for Existing Projects, but Not the Default Choice
The GPL version of DHTMLX Gantt has its place, but today it is mainly relevant for developers who are already working with older releases. If your project is based on Gantt v9.x or earlier, you can continue using the GPL edition available on GitHub.
For new development, however, GPL is no longer the recommended route. The release of the MIT-licensed Community Edition changed the situation by providing a simpler option for projects that need more freedom in how the component is used and distributed.
This does not make GPL unsuitable. It can still be a practical choice when the main goal is experimenting with Gantt functionality, creating educational examples, or maintaining an open-source project that already relies on an older version.
Typical scenarios where the GPL JavaScript Gantt chart can still be considered include:

• learning how Gantt functionality works before starting a larger project;

• building proof-of-concept applications;

• maintaining existing open-source solutions based on previous DHTMLX Gantt releases.
If you are starting a new application and want to work with the latest DHTMLX Gantt versions, the MIT Community Edition is usually a better starting point.
MIT License: More Freedom for Modern Applications
For many developers, licensing is not something they want to think about every time they add a library to a project. This is where the MIT-licensed DHTMLX Gantt Community Edition changes the experience.
The MIT model removes many of the restrictions associated with stronger copyleft licenses. Developers can integrate the component into open-source projects, internal tools, and commercial applications without the requirement to make their own source code publicly available.
Of course, MIT does not mean “no rules at all.” Projects using DHTMLX Gantt still need to preserve the original copyright notice and include the license text according to MIT requirements.
From a technical perspective, the Community Edition covers the core functionality required for implementing a JavaScript Gantt chart. Developers can customize the component through its API, adapt it to application requirements, and integrate it into projects built with different technologies.
This makes the MIT edition a suitable option for:

• products with standard project planning workflows;

• startups that want to validate ideas before investing in additional functionality;

• internal business applications;

• commercial apps that do not yet require PRO features.
Another advantage is the ability to change direction later. If project requirements become more advanced, teams can move from the Community Edition to the PRO edition instead of rebuilding the application from scratch.
PRO License: When Standard Gantt Functionality Is No Longer Enough
Many projects start with basic scheduling requirements. A team may only need to display tasks on a timeline, visualize dependencies, or add a simple planning view to an internal tool. In these cases, the Community Edition can often cover the required functionality.
The situation changes when the Gantt chart becomes a core part of a product. Enterprise applications usually involve more complicated workflows: automatic scheduling, resource allocation, large datasets, advanced task management, and integration with existing business processes. At this stage, having access to ready-made capabilities can save significant development time.
This is where the commercial DHTMLX Gantt PRO edition comes into play. Instead of building complex functionality from scratch, teams get access to a collection of advanced features designed for professional project management scenarios.
The PRO version includes capabilities such as:

• scheduling automation tools;

• resource management features;

• enhanced task handling;

• watermark-free export options;

• framework wrappers for React, Angular, and Vue.
The availability of framework integrations is especially useful for teams working with modern frontend stacks. Instead of creating custom integration layers, developers can use dedicated wrappers designed to work with popular frameworks.
You can check the detailed differences between the Community Edition and PRO edition in the feature comparison guide.
Another factor that often matters for production applications is technical support. Commercial licensing provides access to assistance from the DHTMLX team when teams face complex configuration questions, customization tasks, or unexpected issues during development.
Depending on the selected license plan, organizations can also receive additional maintenance options and other services that help support long-term projects.
For these reasons, the commercial JavaScript Gantt chart version is commonly considered for applications such as:

• enterprise project management platforms;

• complex scheduling and resource planning systems;

• SaaS products and proprietary business applications.
The main advantage of the PRO edition is not just the number of additional features. It is the ability to reduce development effort when a Gantt chart becomes a critical part of a larger system.
Wrapping Up
The best DHTMLX Gantt license depends on where your project is today and where it is expected to go next. For developers maintaining older open-source projects, the GPL version of DHTMLX Gantt remains available for legacy releases. New projects that need a flexible and permissive licensing model can use the MIT-licensed Community Edition, while teams building enterprise applications with advanced scheduling requirements can benefit from the additional capabilities of the PRO edition.
This flexibility allows developers to start with the functionality they need and adopt more advanced options when project requirements become more complex. Whether you are creating a prototype, an internal tool, or a full-scale business platform, DHTMLX Gantt provides a licensing path that can match different stages of development.

This article was originally published on the DHTMLX blog on June 25, 2026.
